summary: I worked as the city engineer in Jacksonville from around 1891 to 1898, during which time the city was expanding rapidly. Back then, Jacksonville had a population between 40,000 and 60,000, quite small compared to today. I was involved in laying out streets, building sidewalks, and installing a new sewer system. The Board of Public Works, including members like Dr. John Lidinglo and James A. Shoemaker, oversaw much of this work. I also recall the construction of the bridge at Brand Street over the railroad tracks during my tenure.

Florida has a rich history of mapping and development that I have studied extensively. I have a large collection of Florida maps, including an 1829 map drawn by Brigadier-General Bernard Anginser showing proposed ship canal routes across the state. The state has been triangulated extensively to aid in accurate mapping and development, including the coastlines and rivers. I have contributed articles to the Florida Historical Society Quarterly about early maps, and I see great potential in new mapping methods like plane-coordination. The state's growth, especially with railroads extending west and south, has been remarkable since the late 19th century.

The St. Johns River was a key feature in early Florida maps and development plans. One proposed ship canal route in 1829 would have followed the St. Johns River from the ocean through Black Creek to the Santa Isabel River. During my time as city engineer in Jacksonville, the city extended its main sewers out to deep water in the St. Johns River. This river has always been important for transportation and infrastructure in the region.

Lake Okeechobee appeared on early Florida maps as part of proposed ship canal routes. The 1829 map I have shows a route going through the St. Mary's River across Lake Okeechobee, aiming to connect to Port St. Joe and the Gulf of Mexico. This was part of early efforts to create a cross-state canal in Florida. Although these plans never fully materialized, they reflect the ambitions of that era to improve transportation and commerce.

The Gulf of Mexico was the western terminus of one of the early proposed ship canal routes across Florida, as shown on an 1829 map in my collection. The plan was to connect the Gulf with the Atlantic side via rivers and lakes, including the St. Mary's River and Lake Okeechobee. These early ideas highlight the strategic importance of the Gulf for shipping and trade in Florida’s development. Although the canal was never built, the concept influenced later infrastructure planning.

I was employed by the Board of Public Works in Jacksonville during the 1890s, serving as assistant and then city engineer. The board members at that time included Dr. John Lidinglo, James A. Shoemaker, and George A. DeOottes, who all served without pay. We were responsible for overseeing major public works like street layouts, sidewalks, sewers, and bridges. I have great respect for those men, believing they deserved more credit than many modern officials who treat their positions politically. The board was central to Jacksonville’s infrastructure growth during that period.

Dr. John Lidinglo was one of the members of the Jacksonville Board of Public Works during my time as city engineer in the 1890s. He served without pay alongside James A. Shoemaker and George A. DeOottes. Together, we worked on important city projects like street planning, sewer systems, and bridge construction. I remember him as a dedicated public servant who contributed significantly to Jacksonville’s development.

James A. Shoemaker was another member of the Jacksonville Board of Public Works during my service as city engineer in the 1890s. Like Dr. John Lidinglo and George A. DeOottes, he served without pay and was deeply involved in the city's public works projects. We worked together on expanding Jacksonville’s streets, sewers, and bridges, including the bridge at Brand Street. I hold him in high regard for his commitment to the city’s welfare.

George A. DeOottes was a fellow member of the Jacksonville Board of Public Works during my tenure as city engineer in the 1890s. He served alongside Dr. John Lidinglo and James A. Shoemaker, all without pay, overseeing important city infrastructure projects. Our work included street layouts, sewer installations, and bridge construction. I remember him as a dedicated and selfless public official who contributed greatly to Jacksonville’s growth.

Dr. Henry Robinson was one of the mayors of Jacksonville during my time working as city engineer in the 1890s. While I don't recall all the members of the various city and county boards, his name stands out as a notable figure in city government. He was part of the leadership overseeing Jacksonville’s development during a period of significant growth and infrastructure improvements. His administration coincided with many of the projects I helped implement.

Brand Street, formerly known as Bridge Street, was the site of a bridge we built over the railroad tracks during my time as city engineer in Jacksonville in the 1890s. This bridge was important for improving transportation in the city, connecting different parts of Jacksonville more efficiently. The current concrete structure replaced the original one I helped oversee. Brand Street was a key location in the city's expanding infrastructure network back then.

The Florida East Coast Railway played a significant role in Florida’s development, especially as it extended west and south, spurring rapid growth in cities like Jacksonville. After working in Jacksonville, I wanted to work for a railroad company in Texas but returned to Jacksonville instead. The expansion of this railway was a major factor in the state's economic and population growth during the late 19th and early 20th centuries. It connected coastal areas and helped open up new regions for settlement and commerce.

My first job after finishing school in England was with the London and North Western Railway. In England, the government owns transportation lines, so the railway system was well planned and efficient. I received thorough and practical training there, which laid the foundation for my later engineering work in America. This experience was invaluable when I moved to the U.S. and worked on various infrastructure projects.

When I first came to America in 1886, I secured work with an engineer in the state of Ohio. The work was very demanding, with long hours and high expectations, which tested my stamina and ability to work under pressure. Although I did not enjoy the harsh treatment, it was a valuable experience that prepared me for future challenges. Later, I left Ohio to pursue opportunities in the South, eventually settling in Jacksonville.

While working in Ohio, I was assigned as an assistant engineer on a government contract to dredge, widen, and improve the Muskingum River. This project was part of my training to handle difficult engineering tasks under pressure. It was a turning point in my career, as it tested my ability to work through challenges and helped me gain confidence. This experience prepared me well for my later work in Florida.

Standard Oil was mentioned in the context of early transportation and industrial development, but I did not have a direct personal relationship with the company. My focus was primarily on public works, railroads, and mapping in Florida and Ohio. While Standard Oil was a major player in the industry, my work was more connected to municipal engineering and infrastructure projects. Therefore, my knowledge of Standard Oil is more peripheral than firsthand.
